title: Single-Cell Transcriptomics Reveals Riluzole as an Osteoarthritis Candidate Drug via OB-NE Signaling Modulation and CTSS/NOS1 Inhibition
research_question: Can drug repositioning, guided by single-cell transcriptomics and analysis of osteoblast-neutrophil interactions, identify novel therapeutic candidates for osteoarthritis?
methods_summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing of human femoral head tissue from OA patients and controls
Integrated bioinformatics analysis including differential expression, GO enrichment, and cell communication analysis (CellChat)
Network proximity-based drug repositioning integrating scRNA-seq data with drug-target and protein-protein interaction networks
Mendelian randomization analysis to assess causal relationships between drug targets and OA risk
In vivo validation using a triclocarban (TCC)-induced zebrafish OA model treated with riluzole
Bulk RNA-seq, network pharmacology, and molecular docking to identify and validate drug targets
dataset: Human femoral head tissue from 3 OA patients and 3 controls (femoral neck fracture patients); zebrafish OA model
key_findings: Osteoblasts (OBs) were identified as key OA-associated cells, with differentially expressed genes enriched in neutrophil (NE)-mediated immune pathways.
Enhanced bidirectional OB-NE signaling in OA was observed, primarily mediated by CXCL12-CXCR4 and RETN-CAP1 ligand-receptor pairs.
Riluzole was identified as a top drug candidate via network-based drug repositioning.
Mendelian randomization showed an inverse causal association between riluzole's target SLC7A11 expression and OA risk (OR=0.84, 95% CI=0.72–0.98, P=0.027).
In a zebrafish OA model, riluzole treatment significantly reduced joint degeneration and downregulated CTSS, NOS1, and key components of the MAPK/ERK pathway.
Molecular docking demonstrated strong binding affinity of riluzole to CTSS and NOS1, suggesting these as potential therapeutic targets.
extracted_insights: This study provides a comprehensive framework for precision drug repurposing in OA by integrating single-cell transcriptomics, cell communication analysis, and network-based drug screening. It highlights riluzole, an FDA-approved ALS drug, as a promising candidate for OA treatment, potentially acting through modulation of OB-NE signaling and inhibition of CTSS and NOS1, leading to suppression of the MAPK/ERK pathway.
